what_i_got_wrong: Wrote hypotheses as 'the slope is/is not statistically significant at the 5% level'
why_i_got_it_wrong: Used vague language instead of formal parameter statements
correct_rule: H0: beta1 = 0 (slope is zero, X has no linear effect on Y). H1: beta1 != 0. Hypotheses are about the parameter value, not about significance.

what_i_got_wrong: Said '95% certainty of rejecting the null hypothesis'
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused frequentist inference with Bayesian probability
correct_rule: Rejecting H0 at 5% means 'sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level'. It does NOT mean 95% probability H0 is false. Never assign a probability to the hypothesis.

what_i_got_wrong: Wrote t critical value as t_{0.0025, 28} instead of t_{0.025, 28}
why_i_got_it_wrong: Added an extra zero — confused 0.025 with 0.0025
correct_rule: Two-tailed at 5%: split alpha = 0.05 into two tails = 0.025 each. Critical value is t_{0.025, n-2}.

what_i_got_wrong: Could not produce the CI formula for the slope from memory
why_i_got_it_wrong: Hadn't memorised the formula
correct_rule: CI = beta1_hat +/- t_{alpha/2, n-2} x SE(beta1_hat). If 0 falls outside the CI, reject H0 (duality principle).

what_i_got_wrong: Could not define SST, SSR, SSE
why_i_got_it_wrong: Hadn't learned the decomposition
correct_rule: SST = sum(yi - y_bar)^2 (total). SSR = sum(y_hat_i - y_bar)^2 (explained by regression). SSE = sum(yi - y_hat_i)^2 (unexplained error). SST = SSR + SSE.

what_i_got_wrong: Could not explain what s_e measures
why_i_got_it_wrong: Got the formula but not the interpretation
correct_rule: s_e measures the typical size of residuals in the units of Y. ~95% of observed Y values fall within +/- 2 x s_e of predicted values. It tells you about prediction accuracy.

what_i_got_wrong: Said heteroscedasticity means estimation is not consistent
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused the consequence — OLS is still unbiased
correct_rule: OLS is still UNBIASED under heteroscedasticity, but standard errors are WRONG, making t-tests and CIs invalid. Detect via fan shape in residual vs fitted plot.

what_i_got_wrong: Could not state the consequence of autocorrelation
why_i_got_it_wrong: Hadn't learned it
correct_rule: Standard errors are UNDERESTIMATED, inflating t-statistics -> spurious significance. Detect with Durbin-Watson (near 2 = OK, below 2 = positive autocorrelation).

what_i_got_wrong: Could not produce a business recommendation from regression output
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know the structure
correct_rule: Three parts: (1) what the data show (significant or not, direction, size with UNITS), (2) the limitation (R^2 not high enough), (3) the action (investigate more variables, proceed with caution).

what_i_got_wrong: Could not independently work through a regression question
why_i_got_it_wrong: Needed step-by-step guidance throughout
correct_rule: Follow the 6-step framework: (1) State model + hypotheses, (2) Interpret slope with units + direction, (3) Compute t, compare to critical value, state decision, (4) Construct CI, check if 0 is included, (5) Evaluate R^2 + s_e, (6) Business recommendation.

what_i_got_wrong: Said non-probability sampling is 'completely left at random'
why_i_got_it_wrong: Got probability and non-probability backwards
correct_rule: Non-probability = researcher judgment or convenience, NOT random. Probability = random selection with known probabilities. Probability is the rigorous one.

what_i_got_wrong: Said clusters are externally heterogeneous
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused strata and cluster composition
correct_rule: Strata: internally homogeneous, externally heterogeneous -> precision. Clusters: internally heterogeneous, externally homogeneous -> cost savings. Sample from ALL strata, only SOME clusters.

what_i_got_wrong: Quota and snowball answers were generic with no business context
why_i_got_it_wrong: Gave textbook definitions instead of applying to the scenario
correct_rule: Every sentence must reference the specific business: name the company, the population, the sampling frame. If the sentence could apply to any company, it is too generic.

what_i_got_wrong: Did not identify the sampling frame gap
why_i_got_it_wrong: Missed the cue in the question about unregistered customers
correct_rule: Always ask: who is in my target population but NOT on this list? For a membership database, casual/unregistered customers are excluded. Flag this gap for every probability method.

what_i_got_wrong: Gave 'strata must be mutually exclusive' as a limitation
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused a property/requirement with a limitation
correct_rule: Real limitations: requires knowledge of stratification variables in advance; sampling frame may not match target population. A limitation is something that could go wrong or restrict usefulness.

what_i_got_wrong: Jumped straight to formula, skipped derivation
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know the 8-step structure was worth ~15 of 20 marks
correct_rule: Follow the 8-step structure: sample -> estimator -> distribution (with CLT) -> CI -> derivation from half-width inequality -> define ALL terms -> calculate -> round up.

what_i_got_wrong: Rounded 96.04 DOWN to 96
why_i_got_it_wrong: Instinct was to round normally
correct_rule: Sample size must always be rounded UP to the next whole number. 96.04 -> 97. Rounding down means you haven't met the precision requirement.

what_i_got_wrong: Wrote e = 3^2 instead of (0.03)^2
why_i_got_it_wrong: Forgot to convert percentage to decimal
correct_rule: When the margin of error is a percentage, convert to decimal before plugging in: 3% -> e = 0.03. Write (0.03)^2 = 0.0009 in the formula.

what_i_got_wrong: Wrote 'incidental %' instead of 'incidence rate'
why_i_got_it_wrong: Misremembered the term
correct_rule: Incidence rate = percentage of people contacted who are eligible. Completion rate = percentage of eligible who finish the survey. Use correct terminology.

what_i_got_wrong: Defined all terms for mean formula but forgot to do the same for proportion
why_i_got_it_wrong: Assumed it was only needed once
correct_rule: Define ALL terms EVERY time: n, z_{alpha/2}, pi, e. The examiner commentary says 'all terms should be defined' in every single year. Do it for both mean and proportion.

what_i_got_wrong: Confused leading with loaded questions
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know the distinction
correct_rule: Leading = sentence structure pushes toward answer ('Don't you think/agree...'). Loaded = emotional adjectives ('excellent', 'reckless'). Different errors, different fixes.

what_i_got_wrong: Missed implicit assumptions in 2 of 3 questions
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't check whether the question assumes the respondent has done the activity
correct_rule: Any question saying 'your last...', 'how often do you...', 'how much do you...' -- check if it assumes the respondent has done it. Fix with a filter question first.

what_i_got_wrong: Said R^2 = r^2 (only true in simple regression)
why_i_got_it_wrong: Applied simple regression rule to multiple regression
correct_rule: R^2 = 1 - SSE/SST. In multiple regression, R^2 measures proportion of total variation explained by all X variables combined.

what_i_got_wrong: Adjusted R^2 formula: used n-k instead of n-k-1
why_i_got_it_wrong: Forgot that you lose k slopes + 1 intercept
correct_rule: Adjusted R^2 = 1 - [SSE/(n-k-1)] / [SST/(n-1)]. Denominator is n-k-1 because you lose k slopes + 1 intercept = k+1 degrees of freedom.

what_i_got_wrong: F-test: said SST/SSE instead of MSR/MSE; called MSR 'mean square residuals'
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused the terms and didn't divide by df
correct_rule: F = MSR/MSE. MSR = Mean Square Regression = SSR/k. MSE = Mean Square Error = SSE/(n-k-1). Not raw sums -- divide by degrees of freedom first.

what_i_got_wrong: Said 'multiple dependent variables' instead of multiple independent variables
why_i_got_it_wrong: Mixed up the terminology
correct_rule: Multiple regression has ONE dependent variable (Y) and multiple independent/explanatory variables (X1, X2, ...). The 'multiple' refers to the predictors, not the outcome.

what_i_got_wrong: Said percentages should be computed in the direction of the dependent variable
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused independent and dependent direction
correct_rule: Compute percentages in the direction of the INDEPENDENT variable, across the dependent variable. Ask: of the people in this group, what % chose each option?

what_i_got_wrong: Calculated df by adding (r-1) + (c-1) instead of multiplying
why_i_got_it_wrong: Mixed up the operation — instinct was to add
correct_rule: df = (r-1) x (c-1). Think: how many cells are free to vary once you know the margins?

what_i_got_wrong: Said point estimate is 'any value in between' the CI
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know that the point estimate is a single specific number
correct_rule: Point estimate = centre of the CI. Calculate as (lower + upper) / 2.

what_i_got_wrong: Said margin of error is the difference between the two endpoints
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused width with margin of error
correct_rule: Margin of error = HALF the width of the CI. Calculate as (upper - lower) / 2.

what_i_got_wrong: Got Type I and Type II errors backwards
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused false positive and false negative descriptions
correct_rule: Type I = false positive = convicting an innocent person (reject H0 when true). Type II = false negative = letting a guilty person go free (fail to reject H0 when false).

what_i_got_wrong: Couldn't set up a proportion test from a word problem
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know how to calculate p-hat or recognise 'at most' as one-tailed
correct_rule: p-hat = count/n. 'At most' or 'no more than' -> H0: p <= value, H1: p > value (upper-tail).

what_i_got_wrong: Didn't know when to use z-test vs t-test
why_i_got_it_wrong: Hadn't learned the distinction
correct_rule: z-test when sigma (population SD) is KNOWN. t-test when sigma is UNKNOWN and you use s (sample SD). In practice almost always t.

what_i_got_wrong: Only partially explained the big-n problem
why_i_got_it_wrong: Knew the mechanics but not the implication
correct_rule: Large n can make trivially small effects statistically significant. Always report effect size alongside p-value. Statistical significance != practical importance.

what_i_got_wrong: Compared Levene's test p-value to 0.01 instead of 0.05
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused significance threshold
correct_rule: Levene's test: compare to 0.05. p > 0.05 = do not reject = variances equal = assumption MET. p < 0.05 = reject = variances unequal = assumption VIOLATED. You WANT an insignificant result.

what_i_got_wrong: Said you can rank which factor matters more by comparing eta-squared when interaction is significant
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't apply the interaction rule
correct_rule: When interaction is significant, the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other. Don't rank main effects. Instead, describe the pattern per segment using the marginal means plot.
